title: Ensure 'Java version' is currently supported (if in use)

input_row.title: Ensure 'Java version' is currently supported (if in use)
input_row.description: Periodically, older versions of Java may be deprecated and no longer supported. Using a supported version of Java for function apps is recommended to avoid potential unpatched vulnerabilities.
input_row.rationale: Deprecated and unsupported versions of programming and scripting languages can present vulnerabilities which may not be addressed or may not be addressable.
input_row.audit: Take note of currently supported versions of Java here: https://www.oracle.com/java/technologies/java-se-support-roadmap.html Audit from Azure Portal 1. Go to App Services or Function App. 2. Click the name of a function app. 3. Under Settings, click Configuration. 4. In the General settings pane, ensure that for a Stack of Java, the Java Version reflects a currently supported release. 5. Repeat steps 1-4 for each function app. Audit from Azure CLI Run the following command to list function apps: az functionapp list For each function app, run the following command to get the Java version: az functionapp show --resource-group --name --query siteConfig.[javaContainer,javaContainerVersion,javaVersion,linuxFxVersion] If Java is in use, ensure the version is currently supported. Audit from PowerShell Run the following command to list function apps: Get-AzFunctionApp Run the following command to get the function app in a resource group with a given name: $app = Get-AzFunctionApp -ResourceGroupName -Name Run the following command to get the Java version: $app.SiteConfig | Select-Object JavaContainer, JavaContainerVersion, JavaVersion, LinuxFXVersion, WindowsFxVersion If Java is in use, ensure the version is currently supported. Repeat for each function app. Audit from Azure Policy If referencing a digital copy of this Benchmark, clicking a Policy ID will open a link to the associated Policy definition in Azure. If referencing a printed copy, you can search Policy IDs from this URL: https://portal.azure.com/#view/Microsoft_Azure_Policy/PolicyMenuBlade/~/Definitions • Policy ID: 9d0b6ea4-93e2-4578-bf2f-6bb17d22b4bc - Name: 'Function apps that use Java should use a specified 'Java version''
input_row.remediation: Note: No action is required if Java is not in use. Remediate from Azure Portal 1. Go to App Services or Function App. 2. Click the name of a function app. 3. Under Settings, click Configuration. 4. In the General settings pane, for a Stack of Java, set the Java Version to a currently supported release. 5. Click Save. 6. Click Continue. 7. Repeat steps 1-6 for each function app requiring remediation. Remediate from Azure CLI Run the following command to list supported runtimes: az functionapp list-runtimes For each function app requiring remediation, run the following command with the appropriate parameters to update the Java version: az functionapp config set --resource-group --name [--java-container --java-container- version --java-version ] [--linux-fx- version ]
input_row.impact: If your app is written using version-dependent features or libraries, they may not be available on more recent versions. If you wish to update, research the impact thoroughly.
input_row.default_value: The version is selected during creation.

step1_initial_assessment:
**AUDIT APPROACH:** Manual

**MANUAL STEPS:**
1. **Navigate to the AWS Management Console**: Open the AWS Console and log in to your account.
2. **Access the AWS Lambda Service**: Since the control pertains to function apps which are similar to AWS Lambda functions, go to the Lambda service.
3. **Select Your Function**: Click on the name of the function you wish to audit.
4. **Check the Runtime**: Under the "Configuration" tab, look for the "Runtime" section. Note the Java version specified.
5. **Check the Supported Versions**: Reference the Oracle Java support roadmap [here](https://www.oracle.com/java/technologies/java-se-support-roadmap.html) to determine whether the Java version being used is currently supported.
6. **Record Your Findings**: Note whether the Java version is currently supported or deprecated.
7. **Repeat for Each Function**: If you have multiple Lambda functions that utilize Java, repeat steps 3-6 for each function.

**Expected secure configuration:** The Java version used must be one that is currently supported according to the Oracle Java support roadmap.

**How to confirm compliance:** If the Java version is currently supported, the function app is compliant. If it is not supported, you need to initiate a remediation process by updating the Java version to a supported release.
step2_review:
**APPROACH VALIDATION:** DISAGREE

- **Manual vs Automated Classification:** The classification as a manual approach is not entirely correct. This compliance check can be automated using AWS APIs, specifically with the AWS SDK for Python (boto3) or AWS CLI.
- **AWS APIs/boto3/CLI Capabilities:** You can use the `boto3` library to list all Lambda functions and their configurations, including the runtime environment. The `list_functions` and `get_function_configuration` methods in `boto3` can be used to retrieve the runtime details programmatically. Similarly, the AWS CLI command `aws lambda list-functions` followed by `aws lambda get-function-configuration` can achieve the same result.

**TECHNICAL ACCURACY:** 5

- **Automation Details:** The original assessment lacks any mention of automation capabilities, which are feasible in this context.
- **Manual Steps Accuracy:** The manual steps are accurate for checking the Java version through the AWS Management Console but are not efficient for environments with a large number of Lambda functions.
- **Program Name Convention:** The assessment does not provide a program name. A suggested name could be `aws_lambda_runtime_java_version_check`.

**IMPROVEMENTS:**

- **Automation:** Implement an automated script using `boto3` to iterate over all Lambda functions and check their runtime versions against a list of supported Java versions.
- **Missing Considerations:** Consider using AWS Config to create a custom rule that checks if the Lambda functions are using a supported Java version. This would provide continuous compliance monitoring.
- **Alternative AWS API Methods:** Utilize `boto3` methods `list_functions` and `get_function_configuration` for automation.
- **Program Name Suggestion:** `aws_lambda_runtime_java_version_check`

**CONFIDENCE:** HIGH

- The assessment can be automated using AWS APIs, and the manual approach is not the most efficient method for checking compliance in environments with numerous Lambda functions.

**FINAL RECOMMENDATION:**

- **Re-classify:** Change the approach from Manual to Automated. Implement a script using `boto3` to automate the compliance check for Lambda function runtimes.
- **Modify:** Include automation details in the assessment, providing guidance on using `boto3` or AWS CLI to programmatically check the Java versions of Lambda functions. Also, suggest setting up AWS Config for continuous monitoring.
